example.name: full_repository_analysis
example.description: Complete analysis of an entire repository with systematic directory traversal
example.context: When you need to perform comprehensive code review across the entire codebase
example.code:
# User request: "Perform a comprehensive code review of the entire repository"
# Step 1: Initialize analysis # - Create tracking structure # - Set up progress markers # - Prepare findings collection
# Step 2: Start with root directory list_files path="." recursive=false
# Step 3: Process each directory systematically # For each directory: # - List all files # - Filter by file type # - Analyze each file individually
# Step 4: For each TypeScript file, perform multi-dimensional analysis: # - Bug identification (null checks, error handling, etc.) # - Performance optimization (unnecessary re-renders, inefficient algorithms) # - Naming conventions (descriptive names, consistency) # - ESLint violations (prefer-const, no-unused-vars, etc.) # - Code smells (long functions, complexity, duplication) # - Security vulnerabilities (injection, XSS, etc.)
# Step 5: Compile findings and generate report # - Organize by directory # - Classify by severity # - Provide specific remediation steps
example.explanation: This example demonstrates the complete workflow for analyzing an entire repository. The mode will: 1. Traverse directories systematically 2. Analyze each file with all required dimensions 3. Track progress to allow resumption 4. Generate comprehensive reports 5. Document all findings for manual review

example.name: focused_directory_analysis
example.description: Analyze a specific directory or set of files
example.context: When you need to review code in a specific area of the codebase
example.code:
# User request: "Review the authentication code in apps/ValyntApp/src/contexts/"
# Step 1: List files in the target directory list_files path="apps/ValyntApp/src/contexts" recursive=false
# Step 2: Analyze each file in the directory # For AuthContext.tsx: # - Check for null pointer exceptions # - Verify proper error handling in async operations # - Review React hooks usage (useEffect cleanup) # - Check for memory leaks # - Verify naming conventions # - Check ESLint compliance # - Identify code smells # - Assess security (token handling, auth logic)
# Step 3: Generate focused report # - Summary of findings for the directory # - Specific issues in AuthContext.tsx # - Recommendations for improvement
example.explanation: This example shows how to focus analysis on a specific directory. The mode will: 1. Only traverse the specified directory 2. Provide detailed analysis of files in that area 3. Generate a focused report relevant to the specific codebase section

example.name: bug_identification_example
example.description: Identify and classify bugs with severity levels
example.context: When analyzing code for potential bugs and issues
example.explanation:
This example demonstrates bug identification with severity classification: - Critical: Null pointer exception (line 5) - application will crash - High: Unhandled promise rejection (line 12) - error handling missing - Medium: Memory leak (line 20) - resource not cleaned up - Low: Inefficient algorithm (line 29) - O(n²) complexity
Each bug should be documented with: - Severity level - Line number - Description - Suggested fix

example.name: performance_optimization_example
example.context: When analyzing code for performance issues
example.explanation:
This example demonstrates performance optimization opportunities: 1. Unnecessary re-renders: Use React.memo for UserList 2. Redundant computation: Cache computed values 3. Unnecessary API calls: Implement caching or memoization 4. Large list rendering: Consider virtualization for large datasets
Each issue should include: - Specific problem description - Impact assessment - Recommended optimization

example.name: naming_convention_example
example.context: When reviewing code for naming consistency and clarity
example.explanation:
This example demonstrates naming convention issues: 1. Ambiguous names: Use descriptive names (userCount instead of x) 2. Inconsistent conventions: Follow project standards (camelCase for variables) 3. Magic numbers: Use named constants (STATUS_ACTIVE instead of 2) 4. Boolean prefixes: Use "is", "has", "can" prefixes 5. Descriptive functions: Use verb phrases (validateEmail instead of handle)
Each issue should include: - Specific naming problem - Recommended naming convention - Example of correct usage

example.name: eslint_violation_example
example.context: When checking code for ESLint compliance
example.explanation:
This example demonstrates ESLint rule violations: 1. prefer-const: Use const for never-reassigned variables 2. no-unused-vars: Remove unused variables 3. prefer-template: Use template literals instead of concatenation 4. no-console: Use proper logging (if rule is enabled) 5. prefer-arrow-callback: Use arrow functions for callbacks
Each violation should include: - ESLint rule name - Line number - Description of the issue - Suggested fix

example.name: code_smell_example
example.context: When reviewing code for maintainability issues
example.explanation:
This example demonstrates code smells: 1. Long method: Break into smaller, focused methods 2. Deep nesting: Use early returns to reduce nesting 3. Duplicate code: Extract common logic into shared functions 4. Tight coupling: Use dependency injection instead of direct instantiation
Each code smell should include: - Type of smell - Specific location - Impact on maintainability - Refactoring recommendation

example.name: security_vulnerability_example
example.context: When reviewing code for security issues
example.explanation:
This example demonstrates security vulnerabilities: 1. SQL Injection: Use parameterized queries 2. XSS: Sanitize user input before rendering 3. Insecure password storage: Hash passwords with bcrypt 4. Missing rate limiting: Implement rate limiting for authentication 5. Insecure direct object reference: Add authorization checks
Each vulnerability should include: - Vulnerability type - Severity level - Specific location - Remediation steps

example.name: progress_tracking_example
example.description: Track progress during large repository analysis
example.context: When analyzing large repositories that require multiple sessions
example.code:
# User request: "Analyze the entire repository"
# Step 1: Initialize progress tracking # Create .analysis-progress file with: # { # "totalDirectories": 45, # "completedDirectories": 0, # "currentDirectory": null, # "findings": {} # }
# Step 2: Start analysis # Process directory: apps/ValyntApp/src/contexts/ # - Analyze AuthContext.tsx # - Analyze other context files # - Update progress: completedDirectories = 1
# Step 3: Continue analysis # Process directory: apps/ValyntApp/src/components/ # - Analyze all component files # - Update progress: completedDirectories = 2
# Step 4: Pause and resume # User interrupts: "Stop for now" # Progress saved: 2/45 directories completed
# Step 5: Resume later # User continues: "Continue code review" # Mode reads .analysis-progress # Resumes from directory 3
example.explanation:
This example demonstrates progress tracking for large repositories: 1. Initialize tracking structure 2. Process directories one by one 3. Update progress after each directory 4. Save progress to allow resumption 5. Resume from last completed directory
Progress tracking enables: - Handling large repositories - Pausing and resuming analysis - Avoiding re-analysis of completed directories

example.name: report_generation_example
example.context: When compiling all findings into a readable report
example.code:
# Code Review Report **Generated:** 2026-01-16T18:55:01.019Z **Repository:** ValueOS **Scope:** Entire repository
## Summary Statistics - Total files analyzed: 156 - Directories processed: 45 - Total issues found: 89 - Critical issues: 3 - High priority issues: 12 - Medium priority issues: 34 - Low priority issues: 40
## Critical Issues (3) ### 1. Null Pointer Exception **File:** apps/ValyntApp/src/contexts/AuthContext.tsx:42 **Severity:** Critical **Description:** Accessing property on potentially null object **Code:** ```typescript const user = getUser(); console.log(user.name); // Will crash if user is null ``` **Fix:** ```typescript const user = getUser(); if (user) { console.log(user.name); } ```
### 2. Unhandled Promise Rejection **File:** apps/ValyntApp/src/services/api.ts:15 **Severity:** Critical **Description:** Async operation without error handling **Fix:** Wrap in try-catch block
### 3. Memory Leak **File:** apps/ValyntApp/src/components/Dashboard.tsx:28 **Severity:** Critical **Description:** Interval not cleaned up in useEffect **Fix:** Add cleanup function
## High Priority Issues (12) ### 1. Security: SQL Injection Risk **File:** apps/ValyntApp/src/services/database.ts:67 **Severity:** High **Description:** Direct string concatenation in SQL query **Fix:** Use parameterized queries
### 2. Performance: Unnecessary Re-renders **File:** apps/ValyntApp/src/components/UserList.tsx:12 **Severity:** High **Description:** Component re-renders on every parent update **Fix:** Wrap with React.memo
... (additional issues)
## Medium Priority Issues (34) - 15 ESLint violations - 12 naming convention issues - 7 code smells
## Low Priority Issues (40) - 25 minor naming issues - 15 code style issues
## Recommendations 1. **Immediate Actions (Critical):** - Fix null pointer exceptions in AuthContext.tsx - Add error handling to API calls - Clean up memory leaks
2. **Short-term Improvements (High):** - Implement security fixes for SQL injection - Optimize React components for performance - Add proper error boundaries
3. **Long-term Refactoring (Medium/Low):** - Refactor long methods into smaller functions - Improve naming consistency across codebase - Add comprehensive unit tests
## Next Steps 1. Review critical issues first 2. Create tickets for each issue category 3. Prioritize security vulnerabilities 4. Schedule refactoring sessions
example.explanation:
This example demonstrates comprehensive report generation: 1. Summary statistics for quick overview 2. Detailed issue listings by severity 3. Code examples showing problems and fixes 4. Prioritized recommendations 5. Actionable next steps
The report format should be: - Easy to read and understand - Actionable with specific fixes - Prioritized by severity - Comprehensive but not overwhelming
